title: Swelling Under Your Cat’s Eye: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat's face swollen? Learn about dental abscesses, stings, and when to seek veterinary care for suborbital swelling in felines.
content_html:
TL;DR: Swelling under a cat's eye is most often caused by a dental abscess or infection and requires a veterinary examination within 24 to 48 hours. Immediate care is necessary if your cat has a fever, is lethargic, or if the eye itself appears clouded or bulging.

What are the common causes of swelling under my cat's eye?

  • Swelling located directly beneath or slightly to the side of a cat's eye is often a clinical sign of a localized infection or inflammatory response.
  • One of the most common culprits is a tooth root abscess, specifically involving the carnassial tooth, where the infection tracks upward and manifests as a bump on the face.
  • Other possibilities include a bite wound abscess from a feline scuffle, an allergic reaction to an insect sting, or, in some cases, a blocked tear duct or a cyst.

Is swelling under my cat's eye considered a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for swelling under the eye is rated as Medium; you should contact your veterinarian for an appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours.
  • While it is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is almost always a sign of significant discomfort or pain.
  • Seek care more quickly if the swelling is accompanied by a high fever, lethargy, or loss of appetite.
  • If the eye itself appears to be bulging or clouded, you should seek immediate care to prevent the infection from spreading to the eye socket or the bloodstream.

How does providing a photo help the triage process for my cat?

  • Providing a clear, well-lit photograph of your cat's face allows a triage professional to assess the exact location and determine if the eyelid itself is involved.
  • A photo allows us to assess the presence of any drainage or "pitting" in the swollen area.
  • Visual evidence helps your vet determine if the issue is dermatological, dental, or ophthalmic, ensuring your pet receives the correct priority level for an exam.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Swelling under a cat's eye can be caused by various factors, including dental issues such as swelling behind the last molar, which may require drainage, warm compresses, and topical corneal lubricants. Neoplasia, while less frequent in cats than in dogs, should also be considered, with eyelid and conjunctival tumors being the most common primary ophthalmic neoplasms and often malignant. Systemic diseases such as Demodex cati, Notoedres cati (scabies), ringworm, immune-mediated skin diseases, feline herpesvirus 1, Chlamydia, Mycoplasma, feline infectious peritonitis, toxoplasmosis, feline immunodeficiency virus, and feline leukemia virus can also manifest with ocular signs, including conjunctivitis and uveitis. Radiographs and ultrasonography of adjacent teeth, sinuses, and the nasal cavity may be recommended to further investigate the underlying cause.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 504)

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human pain relievers for the swelling?
faqs.answer: No. Human medications like Tylenol (acetaminophen) or Ibuprofen are extremely toxic to cats and can be fatal. Only use medications prescribed by your veterinarian.

faqs.question: Could this just be a bug bite?
faqs.answer: It is possible. However, because dental abscesses look identical to insect stings and are much more common in cats, a professional exam is necessary to rule out a painful tooth infection.

faqs.question: What if the swelling bursts and drains?
faqs.answer: If the swelling ruptures and releases pus or blood, your cat may feel temporary relief from the pressure, but the underlying cause still requires antibiotics and treatment to prevent the infection from returning.
